dataset: Home Credit held-out test (61,503 rows, DR=0.0807)

balanced.label: Balanced tradeoff; matches PD semantics (20%=1-in-5 default)

aggressive.label: High approval rate; more risk in green zone

selected_policy.name: balanced
selected_policy.rationale: PD=0.20 means ~20% observed default — semantically defensible zone boundary. Approve zone DR=5.77% (acceptable risk). Review zone DR=26.96% (manual evaluation warranted). Red zone DR=53.77% (high-risk escalation). Calibrated PD justifies threshold choice; not arbitrary.
alternatives_rejected: conservative (lo=0.15, hi=0.35): Approve zone DR=4.88% — marginally safer but approve rate shrinks; review burden grows
aggressive (lo=0.30, hi=0.50): Approve zone DR=6.94% — too many borderline applicants auto-approved; review zone DR=40% impractical
ks-optimal single-threshold (t=0.0961): Best discrimination but collapses three-zone business logic
threshold_limitations: Thresholds derived on public Home Credit data (2007–2011 vintage); transfer to other portfolios or eras untested
No real lending economics — cost matrix is scenario-assumed (C_bad=10, C_reject=1)
Review zone capacity not modelled — operational constraint may require narrower amber band
No demographic-group threshold calibration; uniform threshold applied to all subgroups
Temporal stability of bands not guaranteed without out-of-time validation

confusion_matrix_at_t020.note: pred=1 means PD>=0.20 (flag/reject zone). Approve=TN+FN. FN=defaulters approved (false negatives).

hard_rules: Threshold derived from calibrated Platt probabilities only — raw uncalibrated scores not used for banding
Test set was not used for threshold optimisation — thresholds chosen on semantics + val-set cost sweep
